skill.name: web-search
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to run Tavily and Exa web search, answer, and extraction apps for research workflows.
skill.summary: Run web search, direct answer, and page extraction tasks through inference.sh apps.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: The external command findings are mostly true positives because the skill is designed to run the inference.sh CLI, but most examples are expected for a web research skill. The critical pipe-to-shell finding at SKILL.md line 20 is a real high-risk installer pattern because it downloads and executes remote code. I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, so the skill is not blocked but should not publish without review.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md line 20 instructs users to run a downloaded installer directly through sh. This pattern can execute remote code before users inspect it, so it is dangerous even when the referenced domain appears related to the skill provider.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a direct curl-to-shell install command. This is a well-known high-risk pattern, although no malicious payload is visible in the skill file.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Drive Skill Behavior
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill permits Bash commands for infsh and provides many examples that run inference.sh apps. This is expected for the stated function, but it means user prompts, URLs, and extracted content may be processed by external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples clearly show external CLI execution. The use is aligned with the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of abuse.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Research Services
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill sends search queries and target URLs to inference.sh-backed Tavily, Exa, and OpenRouter apps. This is legitimate for web research, but users need a privacy warning because sensitive queries or private URLs could leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples show queries, URLs, and prompts being submitted to named external apps. This is consistent with the skill description, but the data-flow risk is clear.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The Ruby or shell backtick execution detections point to markdown code fences, inline app names, and table formatting. These locations are not Ruby execution syntax, but actual CLI command risks are reviewed separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown syntax or inline code spans rather than executable Ruby or shell backticks. I verified actual command execution risk separately through allowed-tools and fenced command examples.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections at SKILL.md lines 3, 30, 37, and 149 do not correspond to hashing or encryption code. They appear to be triggered by prose, headings, or table content in markdown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are markdown metadata, table headers, or links, not cryptographic implementation. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or insecure crypto usage was found in those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The hardcoded URL detections are mostly provider links, example URLs, and documentation references. They are network indicators, but I found no evidence that they exfiltrate secrets or contact unrelated domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible markdown links or placeholder example URLs. The installer URL remains high risk due to shell execution, but the URL literals alone are not malicious.

low_findings.title: Related Skill Installation Commands Need User Awareness
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The npx skills add examples install additional skills from the same source family. This can be legitimate discovery content, but marketplace users should understand that those commands fetch and add more code outside this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and easy to inspect, but they delegate installation of other skills. I did not find evidence that the referenced skills are malicious from this file alone.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Install Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads a shell script and executes it immediately. Replace this with a signed package manager flow or a documented manual verification step before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly present on the cited line. The exact remote script contents are not included in the audited file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Research Commands With User Supplied Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples submit queries, URLs, and prompts to remote apps. This is central to the skill, but it requires clear privacy expectations for sensitive research topics.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show user-controlled research inputs being sent through external app commands. The behavior is expected, but the privacy risk is real.

content.value_statement: Research workflows often need current sources, extracted page text, and direct answers. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Tavily and Exa through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Runs Tavily search assistant commands through the inference.sh CLI.
Extracts clean content from one or more URLs with Tavily extract.
Runs Exa search for relevant links and context.
Requests direct factual answers through Exa answer.
Combines search or extraction output with OpenRouter Claude model examples.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, login, network access, and provider availability.
Sends search queries, URLs, and prompts to external services.
Does not verify source accuracy beyond provider results.
Provides markdown command guidance, not a bundled local search engine.

use_cases.title: Current Topic Research
use_cases.description: Gather search results, source links, and concise answers for a fast research pass.

use_cases.title: RAG Source Collection
use_cases.description: Find and extract web content that can feed retrieval-augmented generation workflows.

use_cases.title: Article Extraction and Summary
use_cases.description: Extract page text from target URLs before asking a model to summarize the content.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the web for recent reliable sources about [topic]. Return the main findings and source links.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Page Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract readable content from [URL]. Summarize the key points and list any useful images or source metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Search Providers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Tavily and Exa to research [topic]. Compare the strongest sources and note any conflicting claims.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Research Brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for [topic], extract the top source pages, then create a concise research brief with citations and open questions.

output_examples.input: Search for best practices for building AI agents.
output_examples.output: A concise answer with several source links, short context for each result, and suggested follow-up searches.

output_examples.input: Extract content from two article URLs.
output_examples.output: Clean article text, page titles, relevant images, and a short summary of each page.

output_examples.input: Create a research brief from search results about quantum computing developments.
output_examples.output: A structured brief covering trends, source-backed claims, uncertainty, and recommended next reading.

best_practices: Review provider terms and privacy requirements before sending sensitive queries or URLs.
Cross-check important claims across multiple sources before using them in decisions.
Inspect installer commands and prefer verified package installation methods.
anti_patterns: Do not send private documents, secrets, or unreleased URLs to external research providers.
Do not treat generated answers as authoritative without checking sources.
Do not run remote install scripts without reviewing the source and trust boundary.

faq.question: What providers does this skill use?
faq.answer: It documents Tavily and Exa apps accessed through the inference.sh CLI.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. Searches, extraction requests, and answer generation depend on external services.

faq.question: Can it scrape any website?
faq.answer: No. Extraction depends on provider support, website access rules, and page availability.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. Queries, URLs, and prompts may be sent to third-party providers.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: The skill file is markdown guidance, but it instructs users to run external CLI commands.

faq.question: Why is the audit marked high risk?
faq.answer: The quick start uses a remote installer piped into sh, which can execute unreviewed code.
